metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction between the auto-recheck AC ('1 retry on the SAME patchset') and the no-bot-comment AC: either explicitly carve out the `recheck` trigger comment (e.g., 'system recheck comment is permitted; no hand-back notification comment') or specify an alternative CI re-trigger mechanism that does not post a Gerrit comment.
findings.finding: The auto-recheck mechanism requires posting a `recheck` comment on the Gerrit change (the only available trigger: `gerrit_to_platform.ini.template` maps `comment-added: recheck = verify` so a `recheck` comment re-dispatches gerrit-verify.yaml). The CI-fail AC simultaneously states 'post NO bot comment.' These two constraints directly contradict each other — an implementer cannot trigger the recheck without violating the no-comment constraint, or satisfy the no-comment constraint without omitting the recheck entirely.
findings.location: AC — 'CI-flake auto-recheck (before hand-back)' and AC — 'CI-fail: … post NO bot comment'
findings.suggested_fix: In the CI-fail AC, change 'post NO bot comment' to 'post NO bot hand-back comment; the auto-recheck trigger (a system `recheck` comment on the failing change) is permitted and is not considered a redundant notification channel.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] **AC-1 (CI-flake auto-recheck):** Define the concrete signal/field/pattern that classifies a `Verified -1` as an infra-failure vs test-failure (e.g., specific GitHub Actions job name regex, exit-code convention, or a separate label value), and specify the fallback behavior when the signal is absent or indistinguishable.
findings.finding: The AC-1 conditional 'where the CI signal distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure, only retry infra-failures' references a signal/vocabulary that is never defined anywhere in the plan. The gerrit-verify.yaml workflow casts a single integer Verified vote (-1/0/+1) with no sub-type field; adapter.py's _TAG_SUFFIXES coverage-gap sub-reasons apply only to LLM-Review message bodies, not to Verified. The plan provides no enum of infra-failure indicators (exit code, job name pattern, message substring, or separate Gerrit label), no decision rule for classifying a -1 as infra vs test, and no fallback behavior for the common case when the signal is absent or indistinguishable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-flake auto-recheck (AC-1)
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) specify that the bot inspects the GitHub Actions run conclusion for each job by calling `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/actions/runs?...` and classifying any run where only infrastructure jobs (e.g., matching `runner-setup`, `clear-vote`) failed as infra-failure, with a defined pattern list; or (b) simplify to 'always retry once on any Verified -1' and remove the conditional, since the infra/test distinction cannot be reliably read from the Gerrit vote alone.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the current `gerrit-verify.yaml` emits any structured infra-vs-test signal on `Verified -1`; if not, simplify AC-1 to 'retry once on any `Verified -1`' (or drop the conditional) to avoid specifying a branch that is vacuously false in the current pipeline.
findings.finding: The AC-1 conditional 'where the CI signal distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure, only retry infra-failures' references a signal that does not exist in the current gerrit-verify.yaml pipeline: all CI failures cast an undifferentiated `Verified -1` via a single `WORKFLOW_CONCLUSION` env var with no structured infra/test tag. The conditional branch that would 'only retry infra-failures' is permanently vacuous today — making it a YAGNI hypothetical serving a future CI signal, not the current done-definition.
findings.location: AC: CI-flake auto-recheck (before hand-back)
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'Where the CI signal distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure, only retry infra-failures' with 'retry once on any `Verified -1`' — matching the actual undifferentiated signal. If infra-tagging is planned, capture it as a separate future story rather than a conditional that silently does nothing today.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the concrete endpoint for Autosubmit removal (`POST /changes/{id}/revisions/current/review` with `{"labels": {"Autosubmit": 0}}`), confirm whether the autolander-bot can remove a vote cast by a different account (the agent), and add an AC or note in S1/S3 granting the bot the necessary ACL if cross-account label withdrawal is not permitted by default.
findings.finding: The plan specifies removing 'Autosubmit from all changes in the affected stack' but never names the concrete Gerrit REST API endpoint or request body for label removal. The standard mechanism is `POST /changes/{id}/revisions/current/review` with `{"labels": {"Autosubmit": 0}}` — but 'current' may reference the wrong patchset if the change has advanced, and voting as the bot may require the bot to have previously voted +1 (Gerrit only allows withdrawing your own vote). Auth scope for this action is also unspecified in S3.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-fail AC (second bullet) and Rebase-conflict AC

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a rollback assertion to the Outcome record section: specify the schema state of the marker directory after rollback (e.g., 'prior bot versions treat unknown marker files as no-ops / a migration script removes markers on rollback / markers are versioned with a schema_version field') and confirm no data loss or stuck stacks result from a deployment rollback. (T11-e: back-out path and no data loss on partial failure)
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new persisted marker format (`needs_rebase` markers with `acknowledged` flag in the project-infra state dir) but makes no assertion about the schema state after a deployment rollback. If a rollback occurs after markers have been written (especially with `acknowledged=false`), a prior version of the bot that does not understand the marker schema will encounter unknown files or misparse them, potentially silently skipping re-drive of Autosubmit removal — leaving stacks permanently stuck with Autosubmit stripped but no agent notification. The plan states only forward-operation semantics.
findings.location: Outcome record section; AC: 'Idempotent ordering under retries/restart'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `schema_version` field to the marker shape (e.g., `schema_version: 1`). Specify that older bot versions encountering an unrecognised `schema_version` emit a warning and skip (rather than silently ignore) the marker, and that a rollback runbook step includes either draining acknowledged=false markers before rollback or running a cleanup script that sets acknowledged=true on any marker whose Gerrit state confirms Autosubmit is already absent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fallback retry behavior when CI does not distinguish infra-failure from test-failure (e.g., 'always retry once' or 'skip retry and proceed to hand-back').
findings.finding: The CI-flake AC's conditional phrasing 'where the CI signal distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure, only retry infra-failures' leaves the fallback behavior unspecified: if the CI system does NOT provide an infra-vs-test signal, the implementation has no spec for whether to retry or skip the retry.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-flake auto-recheck AC, sentence 2
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence: 'If the CI signal does not distinguish failure type, apply the retry unconditionally (1 retry on the same patchset).'

findings.finding: The `stack_id` assignment protocol (topic assigned at selection time, fallback to tip change-id) is specified in S3's Outcome record section, but the selection logic lives in S2's `wipChain` — S2's schema (`{change_id, chain_member_ids[], tested_sha, verified_at, phase}`) contains no `stack_id` field and no topic-assignment step. The decision rule for how and when `stack_id` is set is not co-located with the schema that needs it (wipChain in S2), creating a structural gap for any executor implementing the cross-story contract.
findings.location: Outcome record section ('`stack_id` = the Gerrit topic the auto-lander assigns to a stack at selection time'); S2 wipChain schema
findings.suggested_fix: In S2's wipChain schema AC, add `stack_id` as a field set during selection (before rebasing phase), with the same fallback rule currently stated only in S3.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] **wipChain schema extension:** Enumerate the phase value(s) S3 adds to S2's `phase ∈ {selecting, rebasing, awaiting_verified, submitting, idle}` for failure handling (e.g., `removing_autosubmit` or `failed`), and specify which phase the wipChain holds during outcome-recording vs. label-removal so restarts route correctly.
findings.finding: S3 introduces failure-path behavior requiring new or extended `wipChain` phases, but never enumerates them. S2 (the dependency) defined `phase ∈ {selecting, rebasing, awaiting_verified, submitting, idle}`. S3's AC-7 states the outcome is recorded before Autosubmit-removal and that the loop 'stops driving the stack', but never names the phases the wipChain transitions through during failure handling (e.g., `recording_outcome`, `removing_autosubmit`, `failed`). An executor extending S2's loop cannot know whether to add new phases to the existing enum, reuse `idle`, or use a different mechanism.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC-7 (Idempotent ordering); cross-ref S2 wipChain schema (phase ∈ {...})

findings.finding: The `failing_change_id?` population rule is never stated for either outcome type. For `ci_failed` the failing change is the one whose patchset received `Verified -1`, but the plan never says how to identify it when a chain has multiple members. For `needs_rebase` the plan says outcome is 'event-driven on that change/stack' implying the 409-receiving change is `failing_change_id`, but this is implicit. Neither rule is co-located with the schema definition.
findings.location: Outcome record section: `failing_change_id?`; AC-1 (CI-fail); AC-2 (rebase-conflict)
findings.suggested_fix: In the Outcome record section, annotate: `failing_change_id?` — for `ci_failed`: the change_id of the patchset that received Verified -1; for `needs_rebase`: the change_id whose POST /rebase returned 409; null/absent if not applicable.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default detection strategy for infra-vs-test failure in the recheck AC (e.g. 'treat all Verified -1 as retryable unless the plan is extended; or match CI comment body against regex X; or inspect Checks API `state=FAILED` with `message` containing "infrastructure"') so the executor can act without a design round-trip.
findings.finding: The AC 'where the CI signal distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure, only retry infra-failures' defers a real design decision to the executor: no Gerrit label value, CI comment pattern, or API field is named to distinguish infra from test failures. An implementer cannot act on this without stopping to choose a detection strategy.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-flake auto-recheck (before hand-back)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a stated default, e.g.: 'For now, treat ALL Verified -1 as retryable (1 retry); a future AC may narrow to infra-only once the CI system's failure-category signal is identified.' This converts the conditional into a concrete default the executor can implement immediately.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the behavior when a new patchset is pushed to the failing change during the auto-recheck wait window (e.g., 'if the current patchset SHA changes during the recheck wait, abandon the recheck and treat the new patchset as a fresh entry into the S2 loop').
findings.finding: The plan does not specify behavior when a new patchset is pushed to the failing change DURING the auto-recheck wait window. In this race, the eventual CI result (Verified -1 or +1) will arrive on the now-stale patchset SHA that the recheck was triggered for, not the new patchset. The plan's `tested_sha` logic and fresh-Verified definition (from S2: 'Verified +1 on the exact rebased patchset SHA') mean the result will be discarded — but the plan does not state whether the loop should (a) detect the new patchset and abandon the recheck immediately, (b) wait for the result and then detect staleness and re-drive, or (c) treat the new patchset as a Autosubmit reset and re-enter the happy path.
findings.location: AC — 'CI-flake auto-recheck (before hand-back)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the concrete file path (or module) for the `needs_rebase` marker store so S4, S9, and the restart-handler all reference the same location — e.g. add `infra/autolander/state.py` (or similar) to the plan's edit-set.
findings.finding: The plan never names a concrete file path or module for the `needs_rebase` marker persistence store ('project-infra state dir' is the only locator given). S2's file_impact only declares `infra/autolander/loop.py`; no second file for the state-dir writer/reader is named in either ticket. An implementer has no edit target for the persistence layer beyond inferring it lives somewhere under `infra/autolander/`.
findings.location: Outcome record section; AC: 'Persisted state is a reconstruct-from-Gerrit cache'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `file_impact` entry such as `infra/autolander/state.py` (the persistence/marker module) and reference it by name in the Outcome record section, so S4/S9/restart-handler all have an unambiguous import target.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the recheck mechanism (e.g. 'post a Gerrit inline comment containing the text `recheck`' or 'call the Checks API re-run endpoint') in the CI-flake AC so the executor does not need to guess.
findings.finding: The 'auto-recheck' mechanism is unspecified: the plan says the bot performs 'auto-recheck' but does not state how — post a Gerrit comment 'recheck', call a specific CI trigger API, re-add a label, or something else. This is a minor defaultable gap but blocks confident implementation.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-flake auto-recheck (before hand-back)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] **Outcome record / AC-7:** Clarify that `acknowledged` is a single per-marker flag set only after ALL label removals for the stack complete (not per-change), and that on restart, a re-drive unconditionally retries all changes in `change_ids[]` treating 404/409 as no-ops.
findings.finding: The `acknowledged` flag's state-increment placement is ambiguous for multi-change stacks. The plan states 'the writer sets acknowledged=false when recording and true after Autosubmit-removal for every change in the stack completes' and 'on restart, any acknowledged=false marker re-drives the removal to completion'. This creates a structural gap: for a 3-change stack where 2 of 3 label removals complete before a crash, the restart re-drive will attempt all 3 again (relying on 404/409 no-op), but the plan does not specify whether acknowledged is a single per-marker flag set only after the entire batch completes atomically, or whether partial-completion state is tracked. If it is a single atomic flag, a restart after partial removal will redundantly re-drive completed removals — acceptable given 404=no-op — but the plan never makes this explicit.
findings.location: Outcome record section — marker shape / acknowledged field; Acceptance Criteria — Idempotent ordering AC-7
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Outcome record section: 'acknowledged is set true only after the label-removal loop over all change_ids[] in the stack returns without error (or only 404/409 errors); it is never set after a partial batch.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand the 'What' section (or add a 'Scope' bullet) to explicitly enumerate the bounded auto-recheck as a third behavior / sub-step of CI-fail handling, so the deliverable list in the body matches all AC items.
findings.finding: The 'What' section declares exactly 'two failure modes: CI-fail (post-rebase Verified -1) and rebase-conflict (POST /rebase 409)', but the ACs introduce a logically distinct third behavior — bounded auto-recheck before CI hand-back — that is not enumerated in the What description. An implementer scoping work from the What section could miss the recheck sub-path entirely.
findings.location: What section ('Handle the two failure modes...') vs AC 'CI-flake auto-recheck (before hand-back)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, change 'Handle the two failure modes' to 'Handle the two failure modes (three code paths): (1) CI-flake auto-recheck — bounded 1-retry before hand-back; (2) CI-fail full hand-back after recheck still shows Verified -1; (3) rebase-conflict (409) hand-back.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify per-call timeouts (and/or a circuit-breaker) for all Gerrit REST calls (POST /rebase, label PATCH, vote GET) so a slow/down Gerrit degrades gracefully rather than blocking the loop indefinitely.
findings.finding: No timeout, circuit-breaker, or degraded-mode is specified for Gerrit API calls (POST /rebase, label-removal PATCHes, Verified-vote polling). If Gerrit is slow or down, the auto-lander loop can block indefinitely, stalling all stacks and potentially exhausting resources.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-fail and Rebase-conflict bullets; Outcome record section
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'All Gerrit API calls carry a per-request timeout (e.g. 30 s); repeated failures within a stack iteration are treated as a transient error and the stack is skipped for that iteration (exponential backoff), not blocking other stacks.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a RETIRE/cleanup path for needs_rebase markers — e.g. prune markers on successful merge of their change_id, or apply a TTL (e.g. 30 days), so the store doesn't accumulate unboundedly.
findings.finding: The needs_rebase marker store has no retirement/pruning path; markers for resolved, merged, or abandoned changes self-invalidate logically (SHA mismatch is ignored) but are never deleted, causing unbounded accumulation over time.
findings.location: Outcome record section: 'self-invalidating: a marker whose SHA no longer matches…is IGNORED'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'On each auto-lander loop cycle, markers older than N days or whose change is Gerrit-MERGED/ABANDONED are pruned from the state dir.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a testing specification covering: (a) unit/integration tests for the auto-recheck 1-retry bound, (b) the 409→needs_rebase→Autosubmit-removal path for both single and multi-change stacks, (c) idempotent restart re-drive of unacknowledged markers, and (d) at least one live/end-to-end acceptance criterion that exercises the real Gerrit rebase and label-removal endpoints (or explicitly name a child ticket deferring live coverage with a rationale for why the offline substitute is faithful to production).
findings.finding: The plan introduces substantial new testable behavior (CI-fail auto-recheck, rebase-409 handling, idempotent Autosubmit removal, persisted `needs_rebase` markers, restart re-drive) but contains zero testing specification — no unit tests, integration tests, or end-to-end acceptance criteria for any of these paths. This is a leaf ticket with no deferred-testing citation to a child ticket. The auto-recheck boundary (1-retry logic), the 409→needs_rebase path, and the Autosubmit-removal idempotency are all new user-facing failure flows with no stated verification approach. Additionally, all three paths interact with live Gerrit APIs; without explicit live/end-to-end acceptance criteria, the suite could go green on mocked Gerrit responses that never exercise the actual rebase endpoint or label-vote removal, making this a proxy-validation risk.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section (all ACs) — no test plan anywhere in the document
findings.suggested_fix: Add an 'Acceptance Tests' subsection with: 1) unit tests for retry-count logic (inject a fake CI-vote sequence that fails twice vs. once); 2) integration tests against a Gerrit test instance for the 409 path and Autosubmit bulk removal; 3) a restart-idempotency test that seeds an unacknowledged marker and verifies removal is re-driven; 4) explicit statement that live Gerrit end-to-end coverage is either included here or tracked in a named follow-up ticket.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Evaluate dropping the `acknowledged` flag and instead re-driving label-removal unconditionally on every restart for any open `needs_rebase` marker (safe because 404/409 are already no-ops), removing the bespoke two-phase saga pattern that has no existing call-site precedent in the codebase.
findings.finding: The `acknowledged` two-phase flag on the `needs_rebase` marker (writer sets `false`; flips to `true` after label-removal completes; restart re-drives any `false` record) implements a bespoke at-rest saga-checkpoint pattern. The plan already specifies that label-removal treats 404/409 as a no-op, so unconditionally re-running the full removal loop on any open marker at restart achieves the same idempotency guarantee without the extra boolean state. There are zero existing call-sites for this two-phase acknowledged pattern in the codebase (not used by the reviewbot dedup ledger, the reconcile cursor, or any other infra state file), violating Rule-of-Three for a new persistence abstraction.
findings.location: Outcome record section; AC: Idempotent ordering under retries/restart
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the `acknowledged` field from the marker shape. On restart, for every `needs_rebase` marker whose patchset SHA still matches Gerrit, re-run the full `Autosubmit`-removal loop (idempotent via 404/409 no-op). The single-slot upsert key already prevents duplicates.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'S4 already does this' with 'S4 (land command, a sibling deliverable) will derive `merged`/`ci_failed`/`review_failed` live from Gerrit at read time' to correctly flag S4 as a forward-reference rather than an existing codebase entity.
findings.finding: The Outcome record section states 'they are derived LIVE from Gerrit at read time (S4 already does this)' — asserting that S4 (the land command) *already* performs live Gerrit derivation of `merged`/`ci_failed`/`review_failed`. No `infra/autolander/` directory exists; no S4 land-command code exists anywhere in the repo. The epic's own Greenfield note confirms S4 is a NEW, unbuilt sibling deliverable. This 'already does' framing is an empirically-checkable codebase assertion that a Grep/Read refutes.
findings.location: ## Outcome record — 'merged / ci_failed / review_failed are NOT persisted — they are derived LIVE from Gerrit at read time (S4 already does this)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the parenthetical from '(S4 already does this)' to '(S4 — the land-command story — will implement this live derivation; S3 does not need to duplicate it)' to make the forward-reference explicit and remove the false 'already exists' framing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `stack_id` assignment to S2's `wipChain` schema and selection-time behavior, or explicitly call out in S3's Outcome record that S3 itself is responsible for assigning `stack_id` when first writing the marker (and specify the fallback rule there, not just in the key description).
findings.finding: The Outcome record section asserts '`stack_id` = the Gerrit topic the auto-lander assigns to a stack at selection time … it is the shared key across S2/S3/S4/S9.' This implies S2's `wipChain` provides a `stack_id` field. But S2's authoritative `wipChain` schema (from S2's AC) is `{change_id, chain_member_ids[], tested_sha, verified_at, phase}` — it contains no `stack_id` field. The S3 marker schema also includes `stack_id`, yet the field's origin (who assigns it, when, how it is stored in S2's in-memory record) is asserted but unverifiable against S2's spec.
findings.location: ## Outcome record — '`stack_id` = the Gerrit topic the auto-lander assigns to a stack at selection time (falling back to the tip change-id for a single-change stack); it is the shared key across S2/S3/S4/S9'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale sentence to the Outcome record section explaining WHY `needs_rebase` must be persisted: a 409 from `POST /rebase` is a transient API error with no durable Gerrit state (no vote, change field, or label encodes 'rebase conflict'), so it cannot be re-derived from Gerrit at read time — unlike ci_failed (Verified -1 vote), merged (change status), and review_failed (LLM-Review -1 vote) which are all live-queryable.
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for why `needs_rebase` specifically must be persisted while `ci_failed`/`merged`/`review_failed` are derived live. The key justification — that a 409 from `POST /rebase` is a transient HTTP error that leaves no lasting queryable Gerrit state (no vote, no change field, no label encodes 'this change had a rebase conflict') — is absent. Without this rationale, implementers may question or misconstrue the asymmetry.
findings.location: Outcome record section — 'persist ONLY needs_rebase markers'; AC — 'Persisted state is a reconstruct-from-Gerrit cache'
findings.suggested_fix: Add after 'they are derived LIVE from Gerrit at read time': 'needs_rebase is the sole exception because a 409 from POST /rebase is a transient HTTP response — Gerrit retains no vote, change field, or label encoding that a rebase conflict occurred — so it cannot be re-derived after the fact.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (with a spike or by reading g2p source at the pinned SHA 44d5d46) that a `recheck` comment posted by the autolander bot account will trigger a `comment-added` → `workflow_dispatch` event through g2p, and document the result in this story; if bot comments are filtered, specify an alternative recheck trigger (e.g., direct `workflow_dispatch` API call with the required GERRIT_* inputs).
findings.finding: The CI-flake auto-recheck mechanism relies on the bot posting a `recheck` comment that triggers the g2p `comment-added` hook → `gerrit-verify.yaml` dispatch. This is an unverified capability assertion: g2p (lfit/releng-gerrit_to_platform) processes `comment-added` events, but it is unverified whether (a) the autolander bot account's own comments will trigger g2p (many Gerrit hook setups suppress self-triggered events or bot-account events), or (b) whether g2p will correctly dispatch `workflow_dispatch` for a comment from a service account rather than a human contributor. If bot comments are filtered or suppressed, the auto-recheck AC silently fails: the stack gets handed back on every first CI failure, violating the 'zero babysitting' goal.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-flake auto-recheck AC: 'do a bounded auto-recheck — 1 retry on the SAME patchset'
findings.suggested_fix: Instead of relying on a comment-triggered recheck (which depends on g2p hook delivery for bot accounts), have the autolander call the GitHub Actions `workflow_dispatch` API directly with the required GERRIT_* inputs (GERRIT_CHANGE_ID, GERRIT_PATCHSET_REVISION, etc.) — this is the same mechanism g2p uses internally and bypasses any comment-filtering concerns.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the concrete Gerrit REST API call used for auto-recheck (likely `POST /changes/{id}/revisions/current/review` with `message: "recheck"`), verify the autolander-bot account has Comment ACL on `refs/heads/*` in project.config (S1 only grants label-Autosubmit — add a comment-permission AC or reference where it is provisioned), and confirm g2p dispatches on comments from service accounts.
findings.finding: The auto-recheck mechanism is described as 'do a bounded auto-recheck — 1 retry on the SAME patchset', but in this codebase 'recheck' is triggered exclusively by posting a Gerrit comment containing the word 'recheck', which g2p's comment-added hook intercepts and maps to a workflow_dispatch (gerrit_to_platform.ini.template: `recheck = verify`). The plan never names the concrete API call the autolander bot would use to trigger this (POST /changes/{id}/revisions/current/review with message:'recheck' is the standard path), nor does it verify the bot's Comment ACL in project.config — S1 only grants label-Autosubmit to Contributors, not a blanket comment permission.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-flake auto-recheck (before hand-back)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC: 'The bot triggers the recheck by POSTing a review comment (`POST /changes/{id}/revisions/current/review` with `{"message": "recheck"}`) on the failing patchset. The autolander-bot account must hold `[access "refs/heads/*"] read` and implicit comment rights (or explicit `label-...` that enables commenting) — verify this is covered by S1/project.config and add an AC in S1 if not.'

findings.finding: S3 asserts that `stack_id` is 'the Gerrit topic the auto-lander assigns to a stack at selection time' and is 'the shared key across S2/S3/S4/S9', but S2's ticket (the dependency) has no `stack_id` field in its `wipChain` schema, no topic-assignment step in any AC, and zero occurrences of `stack_id` appear anywhere in the repo. S3 relies on a capability S2 does not specify.
findings.location: Outcome record section: '`stack_id` = the Gerrit topic the auto-lander assigns to a stack at selection time (falling back to the tip change-id for a single-change stack); it is the shared key across S2/S3/S4/S9.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to S2's AC: 'At selection time, assign a `stack_id` to the `wipChain`: use the Gerrit topic if one exists on the tip change, else use the tip `change_id` as a string; record `stack_id` in the `wipChain` schema alongside `change_id`.' Then S3's assertion becomes verifiable against S2's own spec.

findings.finding: The plan introduces a new auto-lander service with a persistent 'project-infra state dir' for the outcome record store, but does not enumerate the outside-dir infrastructure files that hosting a new service/volume would require updating: `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml` (new service or volume entry), `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` SITE_SUBDIRS list (new state dir bind-volume), and `infra/scripts/config-check.sh` (drift-gate pairing check). These are all outside `infra/autolander/` and are load-bearing for the state dir to actually persist across container restarts.
findings.location: Outcome record section: 'auto-lander state dir (project-infra state)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `autolander` to SITE_SUBDIRS in compose-up.sh, declare a `gerrit_autolander` external volume in docker-compose.yml mounted at the state dir path, and note these files in the plan's edit-target list.

findings.finding: The Idempotent ordering AC states 'label-removal treats 404/409 as a no-op', but Gerrit's label-removal endpoint (POST /changes/{id}/revisions/{revId}/review with a zero-vote payload) does not return 409 when a label is already absent — it returns 200. The 409 is the rebase-conflict status code, not a label-removal code. This unverified assertion may conflate the two paths and could cause a real 409 from a concurrent submit/rebase attempt during label removal to be silently swallowed rather than surfaced.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Idempotent ordering under retries/restart (last bullet)

findings.finding: The idempotency AC asserts 'label-removal treats 404/409 as a no-op', but POST /revisions/{rev}/review (setReview) does not return 409 on a label-removal call — 409 is the response code for POST /rebase on a conflict. Treating a hypothetical setReview 409 as a no-op is based on an unverified/contradicted assumption about that endpoint's error surface; if setReview does return 409 (e.g. for a concurrent edit conflict on Gerrit's internal locking), swallowing it silently could mask a real failure during Autosubmit removal.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Idempotent ordering under retries/restart: 'label-removal treats 404/409 as a no-op'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the AC to: 'label-removal treats `404` (change/revision not found — already gone) as a no-op; other non-2xx responses are logged and retried per the retry policy.' Remove the unsupported 409 assertion for setReview.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one concrete proving command per AC group (e.g. a test scenario that injects a synthetic Verified -1, asserts the recheck is triggered and the stack's Autosubmit labels are removed; a restart scenario asserting an unacknowledged marker re-drives removal; a grep/jq assertion against the state-dir marker file after a 409).
findings.finding: No proving commands or verification steps exist for any of the seven acceptance criteria. Every AC describes a behavioral requirement but provides no concrete command, test invocation, or Gerrit API query that would produce observable evidence of success. The plan names no test harness, no integration scenario, and no end-to-end check, leaving completeness entirely unverifiable by inspection.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all seven)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC or explicitly document a rationale for deferral: either 'a staging/integration scenario injecting a real 409 and asserting all Autosubmit labels are absent in Gerrit afterward' must be present, or state 'end-to-end exercise is deferred to story SX with rationale Y'.
findings.finding: The new CI-fail and rebase-conflict code paths (the entire new behavior this story introduces) have no AC that exercises them end-to-end against a real or realistic Gerrit instance. All behavioral claims are stated as requirements without a cutover-exercising criterion, so green offline/mocked tests can satisfy the plan without the new paths ever executing in production conditions.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-fail and Rebase-conflict ACs; Scope section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Move the 'no bot comment on CI failure' constraint and its rationale into the What or Scope body (e.g., under a 'Notifications / observability' note), so it is a discoverable design decision rather than a buried AC clause.
findings.finding: The constraint 'post NO bot comment (the native -1 + run logs are the signal — no redundant channel)' appears only inside the CI-fail AC and has no corresponding description or rationale in the What, Scope, or body sections. This makes it an orphan criterion: a verifiable requirement with no body coverage and no rationale for an implementer to find without reading every AC line.
findings.location: AC: CI-fail — 'post NO bot comment'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short bullet under Scope or What: 'Observability: on failure, emit a rolling log line (see Outcome record) but post NO bot comment to Gerrit — the native Verified -1 and CI run logs are the authoritative signal; a bot comment would be a redundant, noisy channel.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a spike or RED-fixture that samples real CI failure signals to validate the infra-vs-test classification logic, and make the retry count (currently hardcoded to 1) a named configurable so it can be tuned without a code change.
findings.finding: The '1 retry' auto-recheck threshold and the infra-vs-test failure distinction are asserted policy choices with no empirical basis or measurement plan. If '1' is wrong (too few retries masks real flakes; too many delays landing), the system silently misbehaves, and there is no stated mechanism to tune or validate this threshold against actual CI data.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-flake auto-recheck (before hand-back)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a configuration key (e.g. `auto_recheck_max_retries: 1`) in the project-infra state config so the threshold is tunable without a code deploy. Add a one-day spike to collect a sample of recent CI `-1` events and verify that the Gerrit/CI signal reliably distinguishes infra from test failures before implementing the classification branch.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a deliverable to produce an ADR or CLAUDE.md section documenting the self-invalidating cache pattern, the `acknowledged`-flag restart mechanism, and the Gerrit-as-authoritative-record invariant so future contributors maintain the contract correctly.
findings.finding: The novel self-invalidating cache pattern (patchset-SHA-keyed markers, `acknowledged` flag driving restart idempotency) is a non-trivial architectural decision with no corresponding ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design-doc update called out in the plan.
findings.location: Outcome record section; Acceptance Criteria — Persisted state is a reconstruct-from-Gerrit cache; Idempotent ordering under retries/restart
findings.suggested_fix: Add a task: 'Write ADR-NNN: auto-lander outcome record pattern' covering (1) why Gerrit is the source of truth, (2) the patchset-SHA self-invalidation rule, (3) the acknowledged-flag idempotency mechanism, and (4) which fields are derived-live vs. persisted. Reference this ADR from S4 and S9 story dependencies.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add observability requirements: instrument ci_failed, needs_rebase, and removal-failure events with at minimum a structured metric/counter (e.g. `autolander_stack_outcome_total{reason=ci_failed|needs_rebase}`) and specify that sustained failure rates trigger an alert.
findings.finding: Observability coverage is limited to a 'rolling per-transition log line'; no metrics, structured traces, or alerts are specified for the new failure paths (ci_failed, needs_rebase, removal failures, restart-driven re-drives).
findings.location: Outcome record section: 'A rolling per-transition log line is kept for debugging'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that marker writes are atomic (e.g. write-to-temp-then-rename, or file lock, or a single-writer design) to prevent torn writes or TOCTOU races when concurrent loop instances process the same stack.
findings.finding: The plan states a re-run 'overwrites' the prior marker for the same change_id+patchset_SHA but does not specify any atomicity guarantee (lock, CAS, or atomic rename) for the write, leaving a TOCTOU window when concurrent loop instances race on the same change.
findings.location: Outcome record section: 'A re-run overwrites the prior marker for the same change_id + patchset_SHA'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'Marker persistence uses an atomic write strategy (write-temp + fsync + rename, or a DB transaction) so concurrent writers never produce a partial/corrupt marker.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one AC with a concrete validation mechanism — e.g., an integration-test scenario (inject POST /rebase 409, assert Autosubmit absent on all stack changes) or an operational metric (alert if Autosubmit remains after a recorded failure marker).
findings.finding: No AC specifies a concrete validation mechanism to confirm the failure-handling behavior works end-to-end; all seven ACs describe system-internal state transitions but none names a before/after workflow comparison, an operational metric, or a test scenario (e.g., inject a Verified -1 and observe Autosubmit stripped from all stack changes).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section (all seven ACs)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the 'rolling per-transition log line' is standard structured logging to the service's log stream (journald/stdout) rather than a separate on-disk file; if it is a separate on-disk append log, either (a) drop it in favour of structured service-log output, or (b) define its retention/rotation policy explicitly.
findings.finding: The plan specifies both a single-slot overwrite marker (the authoritative `needs_rebase` record keyed by `change_id + patchset_SHA`) AND a separate 'rolling per-transition log line for debugging.' Maintaining two parallel persistence representations of the same event (a slot-overwrite file and an append log) is over-engineering: if the log is structureless debug output, it duplicates what the single-slot marker already captures; if it is structured enough to be useful, it is a second authoritative record in tension with the 'single source of truth' design principle. No existing infra state file in the codebase (reviewbot dedup ledger, reconcile cursor) uses this dual-format pattern.
findings.location: Outcome record section: 'A rolling per-transition log line is kept for debugging even though the authoritative record is single-slot.'
findings.suggested_fix: Emit transition debug lines as structured log entries to the service's standard log stream (journald/stdout, consistent with infra/scripts/observability.sh patterns) rather than maintaining a separate on-disk rolling file. The single-slot marker is sufficient persistent state.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the file paths (or planned module names) for the S4 and S9 consumers of the `needs_rebase` marker, and specify the read interface (function name or access pattern) so the write/read contract is explicit and auditable.
findings.finding: S4 (land command) and S9 (batch failure) are named as consumers of the `needs_rebase` marker cache, but neither their file paths nor the interface they will use (which function/key to read, which schema fields they depend on) is enumerated. If S3 changes the marker shape or path, S4/S9 must be updated — but the plan gives no way to find them.
findings.location: Outcome record section: 'S4 (land command) and S9 (batch failure) consume this cache + live Gerrit state as the outcome.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document why Gerrit-native persistence (hashtags or a custom label on the change) was rejected in favor of a local file-based `needs_rebase` marker store, or adopt a Gerrit-native approach that keeps the signal co-located with the source of truth and eliminates the `acknowledged` two-phase recovery complexity.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel bespoke 'reconstruct-from-Gerrit cache' file store (persisted `needs_rebase` markers keyed by `change_id + patchset_SHA`, with a two-phase `acknowledged` flag and a rolling log) as the persistence mechanism, but does not consider whether Gerrit's own native mechanisms — hashtags (`POST /changes/{id}/hashtags`) or a custom label (e.g., `Needs-Rebase`) — could carry the same `needs_rebase` signal without any local file store. Given the plan's own statement that 'Gerrit is the source of truth', storing the signal in Gerrit itself would eliminate the file-store consistency risk (stale markers on host failure, acknowledged=false recovery complexity) while keeping the signal co-located with the change data S4/S9 already read.
findings.location: Outcome record section; AC: 'Persisted state is a reconstruct-from-Gerrit cache'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
